theory.intro: Once a variable is known to be **numerical** or **categorical**, it has a **subtype**. A numerical variable is **discrete** if its values are **counted in whole steps** (number of pets) or **continuous** if they are **measured and can take any value in a range** (height, time). A categorical variable is **ordinal** if its categories have a **natural order** (small/medium/large) or **nominal** if they have **no order** (eye colour, brand).
facts: **Discrete** (numerical): values come from **counting**; they jump in whole steps with nothing in between — e.g. number of goals, seats on a bus.
**Continuous** (numerical): values come from **measuring**; they can be any value in a range, as precise as you like — e.g. height, time, mass.
**Nominal** (categorical): categories have **no natural order** — e.g. eye colour, suburb, brand of car.
**Ordinal** (categorical): categories have a **natural order** — e.g. clothing size (S/M/L), report grade, level of satisfaction.
Careful: sizes like **S/M/L** are ordinal (they order), not nominal; and a **count** stays discrete even when the numbers are large.

foundation.question_text: The variable **number of sheep in Australia** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
The variable **time taken to complete a test** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
foundation.solution_text:
Time is **measured** and can take any value in a range.
**Continuous.**

foundation.question_text: The variable **number of students late to school today** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
The variable **mass of a schoolbag** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
foundation.solution_text:
Mass is **measured** and can take any value in a range.
**Continuous.**

foundation.question_text: The variable **method of travel to work** is categorical. Is it nominal or ordinal?
foundation.solution_text:
Travel methods (car, bus, walk) have **no natural order**.
**Nominal.**

foundation.question_text: The variable **report grade from A to E** is categorical. Is it nominal or ordinal?
foundation.solution_text:
Grades have a **natural order** from A to E.
**Ordinal.**

development.question_text: The variable **suburb of home** is categorical. Is it nominal or ordinal?
The variable **level of satisfaction with the government** is categorical. Is it nominal or ordinal?
development.solution_text:
Satisfaction levels run from low to high, so they have a **natural order**.
**Ordinal.**

development.question_text: The variable **school population** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
The variable **height of a Year 9 student** is numerical. Is it discrete or continuous?
development.solution_text:
Height is **measured** and can take any value in a range.
**Continuous.**

mastery.question_text: The variable **clothing size (small, medium, large)** is categorical. A student says it is nominal because the sizes are just names. Is it nominal or ordinal?
mastery.solution_text:
Small, medium and large follow a **natural order** from smallest to largest.
Because the categories can be ordered, the variable is **ordinal**, not nominal.

mastery.question_text: The variable **number of people at a concert** is numerical. Because the number is very large, a student says it is continuous. Is it discrete or continuous?
mastery.solution_text:
The people are **counted** in whole steps — you cannot have half a person.
A large count is still counted, so the variable is **discrete**, not continuous.

mastery.question_text: Zach classifies **age** as discrete; Ed classifies it as continuous. Explain how each could be correct.
mastery.solution_text:
If age is recorded as a whole number of years (a count), it is **discrete**.
If age is measured exactly — to the day, hour or second — it can take any value, so it is **continuous**.
Both are correct, depending on how age is recorded.
